title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Tom Ellis, who presents a morning radio programme.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Tom Ellis, who presents a morning radio programme. Tom, how did you get your first radio job? || Guest: I loved music, but I didn't know anyone in radio. I volunteered at a hospital radio station while I was at college. [20]A local presenter heard me there and invited me to help with weekend shows at a small station[/20]. I made tea, answered phones and slowly learnt what happened in a real studio. || Interviewer: What was difficult at the beginning? || Guest: I thought speaking would be the hard part, but I was comfortable with that. [21]The difficult thing was learning to listen carefully while preparing the next item at the same time[/21]. On radio you cannot stop and think for long. You need to hear what your guest says, watch the clock and get ready for the news. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about morning radio? || Guest: It is early, and the alarm is painful, but [22]I like knowing that people are starting their day with us in the kitchen or in the car[/22]. Listeners send messages about breakfast, traffic or children going to school. It feels friendly, even though I may never meet most of them. || Interviewer: How do you choose music for the programme? || Guest: We play some new songs, but mornings are not the time for anything too strange. [23]I try to choose music that gives people energy without annoying them before work[/23]. A song might be excellent, but if it is too loud or complicated at seven o'clock, it can wait until later in the day. || Interviewer: Have you ever made a mistake on air? || Guest: Many times. Once I read the weather for the wrong town and received lots of messages. [24]I learnt that admitting a mistake quickly is better than pretending it did not happen[/24]. Listeners are usually kind if you are honest, and sometimes the mistake becomes the funniest part of the show. || Interviewer: What would you like to do in the future? || Guest: I'd like to make more programmes outside the studio. [25]Recording in markets, schools and small businesses would let listeners hear real voices from the community[/25]. A studio is comfortable, but radio should not feel distant. I want people to recognise their own lives when they listen. || Interviewer: Do you still get nervous before a live show? || Guest: A little, and I think that is healthy. Live radio should feel alive. You prepare as much as possible, but you never know exactly what a guest will say or what story might break during the programme. That uncertainty keeps me awake better than coffee. The trick is not to sound nervous, because listeners want company, not stress. I take a few quiet minutes before we start, check the first links, and remind myself that the audience is usually on our side. I also enjoy mentoring students who visit the station. They often arrive wanting to be famous, but they leave understanding that good radio is mainly about curiosity and careful preparation.  That is a lesson I still try to remember before every programme.  Preparation makes the live moments easier.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Tom Ellis, who presents a morning radio programme. Tom, how did you get your first radio job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I loved music, but I didn't know anyone in radio. I volunteered at a hospital radio station while I was at college. A local presenter heard me there and invited me to help with weekend shows at a small station. I made tea, answered phones and slowly learnt what happened in a real studio.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult at the beginning?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I thought speaking would be the hard part, but I was comfortable with that. The difficult thing was learning to listen carefully while preparing the next item at the same time. On radio you cannot stop and think for long. You need to hear what your guest says, watch the clock and get ready for the news.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about morning radio?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It is early, and the alarm is painful, but I like knowing that people are starting their day with us in the kitchen or in the car. Listeners send messages about breakfast, traffic or children going to school. It feels friendly, even though I may never meet most of them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you choose music for the programme?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We play some new songs, but mornings are not the time for anything too strange. I try to choose music that gives people energy without annoying them before work. A song might be excellent, but if it is too loud or complicated at seven o'clock, it can wait until later in the day.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you ever made a mistake on air?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many times. Once I read the weather for the wrong town and received lots of messages. I learnt that admitting a mistake quickly is better than pretending it did not happen. Listeners are usually kind if you are honest, and sometimes the mistake becomes the funniest part of the show.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like to do in the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'd like to make more programmes outside the studio. Recording in markets, schools and small businesses would let listeners hear real voices from the community. A studio is comfortable, but radio should not feel distant. I want people to recognise their own lives when they listen.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you still get nervous before a live show?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: A little, and I think that is healthy. Live radio should feel alive. You prepare as much as possible, but you never know exactly what a guest will say or what story might break during the programme. That uncertainty keeps me awake better than coffee. The trick is not to sound nervous, because listeners want company, not stress. I take a few quiet minutes before we start, check the first links, and remind myself that the audience is usually on our side. I also enjoy mentoring students who visit the station. They often arrive wanting to be famous, but they leave understanding that good radio is mainly about curiosity and careful preparation.  That is a lesson I still try to remember before every programme.  Preparation makes the live moments easier.

questions.question: How did Tom get his first chance in local radio?

options.A: He won a competition for young presenters.
options.B: A presenter heard him on hospital radio.
options.C: His college arranged work experience for him.

questions.explanation: Tom says a local presenter heard him at hospital radio and invited him to help.

questions.question: What did Tom find difficult at first?
questions.explanation: He says the difficult thing was listening carefully while preparing the next item.

questions.question: What does Tom enjoy about morning radio?

options.C: being part of listeners' daily routines

questions.explanation: Tom likes knowing people start their day with the programme at home or in the car.

questions.question: How does Tom choose music for the morning?

options.B: He avoids music that may irritate listeners.
options.C: He asks listeners to vote each day.

questions.explanation: He chooses music that gives energy without annoying people before work.

questions.question: What did Tom learn after making a mistake?

options.C: to avoid reading the weather live

questions.explanation: Tom learnt that admitting a mistake quickly is better than pretending it did not happen.

questions.question: What kind of programme would Tom like to make?

options.B: a later programme for young people

questions.explanation: Tom wants to record in markets, schools and small businesses so listeners hear community voices.
